en.title: Shivaji Maharaj: The Life of a Warrior King
en.summary: A cinematic account of young Shivaji building forts, alliances, and a new idea of swarajya.

chapters.title: The Deccan Before Shivaji
chapters.content:
To understand the scale of what Shivaji Maharaj achieved, one must first understand the fractured, foreign-dominated world he was born into.

By the early seventeenth century, the Deccan plateau — the vast tableland covering much of present-day Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Telangana — was divided among three major Muslim sultanates, remnants of the older Bahmani Sultanate that had splintered in the late 1400s:

Ahmadnagar Sultanate (Nizamshahi dynasty), controlling much of western Maharashtra
Bijapur Sultanate (Adilshahi dynasty), controlling the southwestern Deccan and parts of Karnataka
Golconda Sultanate (Qutbshahi dynasty), controlling the eastern Deccan around present-day Hyderabad

To the north, the Mughal Empire, then at the height of its power under Emperor Shah Jahan (and, from 1658, Aurangzeb), was steadily extending its reach southward, absorbing Ahmadnagar outright by 1636 and applying constant military and diplomatic pressure on Bijapur and Golconda.

The Marathas — Marathi-speaking communities native to the Sahyadri (Western Ghats) hill country and the Konkan coastal strip — held no independent political power of their own. Instead, Maratha families served as *jagirdars* (holders of land grants in exchange for military service), *deshmukhs* (hereditary local administrators and revenue collectors), and cavalry commanders under the various sultanates. Prominent Maratha clans — the Bhosales, Jadhavs, Mores, Ghatges, Nimbalkars, and others — often fought on opposing sides of the same conflict, serving whichever sultan employed them, and sometimes fighting each other.

This was a world defined by shifting loyalty, court intrigue, and the near-total absence of a unified regional identity or Hindu political sovereignty in the Deccan — a condition that had persisted for centuries since the fall of earlier Hindu kingdoms in the region. It is against this backdrop of fragmentation that Shivaji's later achievement — carving out and formally crowning an independent Hindu kingdom — takes on its full historical significance.

chapters.title: Ancestry and Family Background
chapters.content:
Shivaji's family, the Bhosale clan, claimed descent from the Sisodia Rajputs of Mewar, though this genealogical claim, common among Maratha noble families seeking Kshatriya (warrior-caste) legitimacy, is treated with some skepticism by modern historians and is best understood as part of the broader tradition of lineage-building among Deccan military families of the period.

His grandfather, Maloji Bhosale, rose from a relatively modest background to become a significant jagirdar under the Ahmadnagar Sultanate, acquiring the forts and revenue rights of Pune and Supe, and being honored with the title "Raja" by the Nizamshahi court. This upward mobility set the stage for his son, Shahaji, to become one of the most powerful Maratha generals of his generation.

Shahaji Bhosale, Shivaji's father, was an exceptionally capable military commander who served — at different points in his career — the Ahmadnagar Sultanate, the Mughal Empire, and finally the Bijapur Sultanate, reflecting the fluid, transactional nature of Maratha military service at the time. After the Mughal conquest of Ahmadnagar in the 1630s, Shahaji attempted for a period to keep the Nizamshahi dynasty alive by installing a young prince as a puppet ruler and continuing resistance from the fort of Mahuli, before eventually being forced to submit and enter Bijapur's service around 1636. Bijapur granted him a substantial jagir in the Pune region as well as territories in the Karnataka region (Bangalore), where he would spend much of his later career.

Shivaji's mother, Jijabai, came from the Jadhav clan of Sindkhed, another prominent Maratha military family that had, at various points, served the Nizamshahi and Mughal courts. She was known for her deep religious devotion, her strong character, and her insistence on maintaining dignity and Hindu tradition even under difficult circumstances — qualities that would shape her son profoundly.

Shahaji and Jijabai's marriage, arranged in the customary manner between allied military families, produced several children, though many did not survive infancy — a common reality of the era. Shivaji, born in 1630, was among the survivors, along with an elder brother, Sambhaji (not to be confused with Shivaji's own son of the same name), who was raised primarily with Shahaji in the Bangalore jagir and was later killed in battle in the Deccan in 1654 while serving under Bijapur.

chapters.title: Birth at Shivneri (1630)
chapters.content:
Shivaji was born on 19 February 1630 at the hill-fort of Shivneri, situated near the town of Junnar in the rugged terrain northwest of Pune. The precise date is a matter of some scholarly discussion, as traditional Marathi sources record it according to the lunar Hindu calendar (Falgun Vadya Tritiya, Shaka year 1551), which corresponds to slightly different Gregorian dates depending on the calculation method used; 19 February 1630 is the date most widely accepted by modern historians and is officially observed in Maharashtra as Shivaji Jayanti.

The choice of Shivneri as the birthplace was circumstantial: with Shahaji frequently embroiled in military campaigns and political instability across the region (this was the same turbulent period in which the Ahmadnagar Sultanate was collapsing under Mughal pressure), Jijabai had taken refuge at the secure hill-fort, which was under Shahaji's jurisdiction at the time.

According to tradition, Jijabai prayed for a healthy son to the local guardian deity of the fort, the goddess Shivai Devi, and named her newborn son in her honor. A temple to Shivai Devi still exists at Shivneri fort today, and the fort itself has been preserved as a protected historical monument, drawing large numbers of visitors, particularly around Shivaji Jayanti celebrations.

chapters.title: Childhood Under Jijabai and Dadoji Kondadev
chapters.content:
Shahaji's career took him increasingly toward Bijapur's service in the Karnataka region, and around 1636, he arranged for Jijabai and the young Shivaji to be settled at Pune, a jagir under his control that had suffered devastation during the wars of the preceding decade. He appointed a trusted and highly capable administrator, Dadoji Kondadev, to manage the estate and oversee Shivaji's upbringing and education.

Jijabai's role in shaping Shivaji's character is regarded by virtually all historians and traditional accounts as the central formative influence of his early life. Widowed of a normal domestic life by her husband's near-permanent absence and the demands of managing a war-torn jagir, she devoted herself to raising Shivaji with a strong moral and religious grounding. She is said to have narrated to him extensively from the Ramayana, the Mahabharata, and stories of historical Hindu kings and warriors, instilling values of righteous rule (*dharma*), courage, and protection of one's people and faith. She also maintained close ties with local temples and religious figures, reinforcing a strong Hindu cultural identity in her son at a time when temple destruction and religious discrimination were recorded occurrences under some regional Muslim rulers, though policies varied significantly by ruler and period.

Dadoji Kondadev managed the practical rebuilding of the Pune jagir — resettling farmers, restoring irrigation and agriculture, and re-establishing law and order in the region, which had been devastated by years of warfare between the Nizamshahi, Bijapur, and Mughal forces. Under his supervision, Shivaji received training in reading and writing, horsemanship, swordsmanship, wrestling, and the fundamentals of statecraft and administration. Some traditional accounts also describe formal instruction in Sanskrit and religious texts, though the extent of Shivaji's formal literacy is debated among historians, as some contemporary sources suggest he could not read or write fluently and relied on scribes — this did not, however, limit his exceptional command of governance and strategy.

Importantly, Shivaji's childhood was not confined to court life. He spent considerable time among the Maval region's hardy hill communities — farmers, shepherds, and hunters accustomed to the difficult Sahyadri terrain — forming close personal friendships with boys such as Tanaji Malusare, Yesaji Kank, Baji Pasalkar, Suryaji Malusare, and others. These early bonds, formed on the basis of shared upbringing rather than caste or hereditary rank, became the foundation of intense personal loyalty that Shivaji would draw upon throughout his military campaigns. This grassroots familiarity with both the terrain and the common people of the region — unusual for a noble's son — is frequently cited by historians as a key factor distinguishing Shivaji's later military approach from that of conventional Deccan nobility.

chapters.title: Marriage and Adolescence
chapters.content:
In accordance with the customs of the time, Shivaji was married at a young age — around 1640, at approximately age ten — to Saibai, from the Nimbalkar family, in a alliance arranged by his parents. Saibai would later become the mother of his heir, Sambhaji.

Through his early-to-mid teenage years, Shivaji continued his education and training under Dadoji Kondadev while spending increasing amounts of time surveying the hill forts and terrain of the Maval region, accompanied by his growing circle of loyal companions. He is described in various accounts as having developed, even before the age of fifteen, a clear sense of resentment at the subjugated political condition of the Marathas and the broader Deccan under foreign sultanates, and a growing ambition to establish independent Maratha rule.

Dadoji Kondadev died around 1647, after which the young Shivaji — by then approaching his late teens — assumed direct control over the administration of his father's jagir and the growing band of followers he had assembled, marking his effective transition into independent leadership.

chapters.title: The Founding of Swarajya (1645–1647)
chapters.content:
The event most widely regarded as the symbolic founding of Swarajya ("self-rule") took place in 1645, when Shivaji — then around fifteen years old — along with his band of Maval companions, captured the hill-fort of Torna from its Bijapur garrison, reportedly through stealth and persuasion rather than a pitched battle, exploiting lax security and winning over or bypassing the garrison.

This was followed shortly after by the capture of the nearby fort of Kondana, which Shivaji had rebuilt and strengthened; it was later renamed Rajgad ("King's Fort") and would serve as his capital for roughly the next quarter-century, until the shift to Raigad. Around the same period, he also gained control of Purandar fort, one of the most strategically important strongholds in the region, through a combination of alliance-building with the fort's existing custodians (the Nimbalkar family, connected to Shivaji through marriage) and assertive action following internal disputes among the fort's claimants.

With these forts secured, Shivaji began establishing an independent administrative structure over the surrounding Maval territory — collecting revenue, maintaining order, and building a loyal cadre of officials and soldiers drawn largely from local families rather than hereditary nobility, a meritocratic approach that would remain a hallmark of his governance throughout his life.

These early actions did not go unnoticed. Shahaji, still in Bijapur's service in Karnataka, faced suspicion and pressure from the Bijapur court over his son's activities, contributing to tensions that culminated in Shahaji's brief imprisonment by Bijapur's regime in 1648, on charges related to disloyalty (also connected to unrelated political intrigues at the Bijapur court). Shahaji was released in 1649 after negotiations, reportedly aided by intervention from Mughal quarters and assurances regarding Shivaji's conduct, but the episode illustrates the precariousness of the Bhosale family's position during Shivaji's rise.

chapters.title: Expansion Through the 1650s
chapters.content:
Following Shahaji's release, Shivaji continued his expansion with somewhat greater caution, avoiding direct provocation of Bijapur while steadily consolidating his hold over the Pune region and pushing into new territory.

A significant campaign of this period was the conquest of the Javali valley in 1656, ruled by the Maratha chieftain Chandrarao More, a powerful and largely autonomous local lord. Following a dispute and subsequent conflict — details of which vary between sympathetic and critical historical accounts, with some describing treachery involved in More's death — Shivaji annexed Javali, gaining control of a strategically vital region that commanded key passes between the Deccan plateau and the Konkan coast. This conquest gave Shivaji access to the site later developed into the formidable fort of Pratapgad, and dramatically expanded both his territory and his reputation as a rising power to be reckoned with.

During this period, Shivaji also began extending his influence into parts of the Konkan coastal region, capturing forts and establishing a presence that would later support his naval ambitions. He captured additional forts including Rohira, Chakan, Lohagad, and others across the Pune and Konkan districts, steadily building the extensive network of hill forts that would remain the backbone of Maratha defense throughout his reign.

By the end of the 1650s, Shivaji commanded a substantial and well-organized territory, an increasingly professional army, and had begun to be perceived — both by rival powers and by the broader Maratha community — not merely as a local jagirdar's ambitious son, but as an independent political force in his own right.

chapters.title: The Killing of Afzal Khan (1659)
chapters.content:
By 1659, Shivaji's growing power had become an urgent concern for the Bijapur Sultanate, then under the regency government following the illness of Sultan Adil Shah II. The Bijapur court dispatched Afzal Khan, a senior and physically formidable general with a substantial military record, at the head of a large army — variously estimated between 10,000 and 12,000 troops — with orders to subdue Shivaji, by capture or, per several accounts, assassination if opportunity arose.

Recognizing that his own forces could not match Afzal Khan's army in open battle, Shivaji withdrew to the newly constructed fort of Pratapgad, set amid dense forest and difficult terrain that neutralized the advantage of Bijapur's larger, more conventionally organized force, including cavalry and artillery poorly suited to the hills.

After a period of maneuvering, negotiations were arranged for a personal meeting between Shivaji and Afzal Khan at the base of Pratapgad on 10 November 1659, under an agreement that each leader would be accompanied only by a small, lightly armed escort as a gesture of mutual trust.

The exact sequence of events at this meeting remains one of the most historically contested episodes of Shivaji's career, with Maratha-aligned chronicles (bakhars) and Persian-language Bijapur court histories offering differing narratives:

Maratha accounts describe Afzal Khan attempting to kill Shivaji during their embrace, either through a concealed dagger or by attempting to crush him bodily, prompting Shivaji — who had anticipated treachery and worn concealed chain-mail armor beneath his clothing — to defend himself using a wagh nakh (a claw-like weapon worn on the fingers) and a concealed dagger (*bichwa*), fatally wounding Khan.
Persian-language Bijapur accounts frame the encounter differently, generally placing greater initiative on Shivaji's side.

What is not disputed is the outcome: Afzal Khan died at or shortly after the meeting, and in the chaos that followed, Shivaji's forces — positioned in ambush across the surrounding hills in anticipation of exactly this scenario — launched a coordinated assault on the leaderless Bijapur army below. The resulting Battle of Pratapgad was a decisive Maratha victory, with large numbers of Bijapur troops killed or captured, and substantial war material, elephants, horses, and treasure seized.

This victory had an outsized impact on Shivaji's standing. It eliminated one of Bijapur's most capable generals, dealt a severe blow to Bijapur's military prestige, dramatically boosted Maratha recruitment and morale, and marked Shivaji's transition from a regional challenger to a major power whose name was now known and feared across the Deccan.

chapters.title: Conflict with the Mughals Begins (1660–1663)
chapters.content:
Shivaji's expanding power inevitably drew the attention of the Mughal Empire, whose Deccan ambitions under the newly enthroned Emperor Aurangzeb (who had secured the throne in 1658 following a war of succession against his brothers) were accelerating.

Bijapur, having suffered the loss of Afzal Khan and further reverses at Shivaji's hands (including further engagements through 1660), and unable to independently contain him, found itself drawn into loose coordination with Mughal forces against the common Maratha threat, though the Bijapur-Mughal relationship itself remained one of mutual suspicion.

In 1660, Aurangzeb appointed his maternal uncle, Shaista Khan, a senior and experienced Mughal noble, as governor (subahdar) of the Deccan, tasking him with the suppression of Shivaji. Shaista Khan occupied Pune — Shivaji's own boyhood home territory — establishing his headquarters at the Lal Mahal, the very residence where Shivaji had grown up under Jijabai's care, and began a sustained campaign against Maratha territory, capturing several forts over the following two years.

chapters.title: The Raid on Shaista Khan (1663)
chapters.content:
In one of the most audacious episodes of his career, Shivaji personally led a small band of hand-picked men in a night infiltration of Shaista Khan's own headquarters at Lal Mahal in Pune on the night of 5–6 April 1663, during wedding festivities in the town that provided useful cover and distraction.

Exploiting his intimate childhood knowledge of the residence's layout, Shivaji's party breached the compound's defenses and reached Shaista Khan's private chambers. In the ensuing confusion and fighting, Shaista Khan escaped serious injury only by fleeing through a window, though he lost several fingers to a sword strike in the process. One of his sons and a number of guards were killed in the raid before Shivaji's men withdrew successfully.

The psychological and political impact of the raid far outweighed its immediate military significance: a Mughal governor and imperial uncle had been struck down within his own heavily guarded headquarters, in territory the Mughals considered secured. The humiliation was severe enough that Aurangzeb had Shaista Khan transferred away from the Deccan in disgrace shortly afterward, reassigning him to Bengal.

chapters.title: The Sack of Surat (1664)
chapters.content:
Seeking both to replenish his treasury after years of continuous campaigning and to further demonstrate Maratha reach and audacity, Shivaji led a major raid on Surat in January 1664 — at the time one of the wealthiest port cities in the Mughal Empire and a major center of international trade, hosting merchants and trading factories of various European companies, including the English East India Company.

Over several days, Shivaji's forces systematically extracted enormous wealth in gold, silver, jewels, and valuable goods from the city's merchants and treasuries, while reportedly sparing certain religious sites and, according to various accounts, showing restraint toward some categories of non-combatants, consistent with Shivaji's broader documented policy of avoiding indiscriminate destruction even during punitive raids.

The raid was a severe blow to Mughal prestige, striking at the empire's commercial heartland and demonstrating that even major imperial cities were not safe from Maratha reach. The substantial plunder also provided crucial funding for Shivaji's ongoing military and naval expansion.

chapters.title: The Siege of Purandar and Treaty (1665)
chapters.content:
Aurangzeb, increasingly alarmed by the string of setbacks in the Deccan, dispatched one of the Mughal Empire's most capable and trusted generals, the Rajput ruler Mirza Raja Jai Singh I of Amber, along with the seasoned commander Diler Khan, at the head of a large, well-resourced army in 1665, with explicit orders to decisively crush Shivaji.

Jai Singh pursued a methodical strategy, combining military pressure with diplomacy, and laid siege to Shivaji's stronghold of Purandar. The defense of Purandar's subsidiary fort, Vasantgad, saw fierce fighting, and the broader siege included the notable last stand of Shivaji's commander Murarbaji Deshpande, who died defending the fort against overwhelming odds after refusing offers to surrender — an episode still remembered as one of the era's notable acts of valor.

Recognizing that continued resistance against the combined weight of Jai Singh's forces was untenable in the near term, Shivaji entered negotiations, resulting in the Treaty of Purandar, signed in June 1665. Under its terms:

Shivaji ceded 23 of his forts to the Mughals, retaining only 12 key strongholds.
He agreed to become a Mughal tributary/vassal, with an obligation to assist Mughal military campaigns, particularly against Bijapur.
His young son Sambhaji was made a Mughal mansabdar (a ranked noble with an assigned position at court/military establishment) as a further guarantee of the agreement.

This treaty represented the most significant political setback of Shivaji's career to that point, though it also bought crucial time and preserved the core of his kingdom intact.

chapters.title: The Journey to Agra and Imprisonment (1666)
chapters.content:
As part of the arrangements following the Treaty of Purandar, and at the encouragement of Jai Singh — who assured him of honorable treatment and safe conduct — Shivaji traveled to the Mughal court at Agra in 1666 to meet Emperor Aurangzeb in person, likely hoping to negotiate more favorable terms, formal recognition, or a Mughal mansab (official rank) appropriate to his stature.

The visit, held on 12 May 1666, went badly. At the formal court audience, Shivaji found himself placed among nobles of a rank he considered far beneath his actual standing and achievements, receiving what he perceived as a deliberate public slight. He reportedly protested this treatment openly before the assembled court, an outburst that further strained an already delicate situation and caused visible offense to the emperor.

In the aftermath, Shivaji was placed under house arrest in Agra, with his movements restricted and a Mughal guard posted around his residence. Contemporary and later accounts suggest that Aurangzeb and his advisors debated Shivaji's fate, with the possibility of execution reportedly considered, given the threat he represented and the humiliation already suffered by the Mughals at his hands.

chapters.title: The Escape from Agra (August 1666)
chapters.content:
Confined but not yet condemned, Shivaji began orchestrating an escape. According to the most widely accepted account, he feigned serious illness, and as part of an apparent effort to seek divine intervention for his recovery, began sending out large baskets of sweets (*mithai*) and fruits daily as religious offerings to Brahmins, temples, and holy men in the city — a practice the guards, over time, grew accustomed to and ceased inspecting closely.

On the day of the escape — traditionally dated to 17 August 1666 — Shivaji and his young son Sambhaji concealed themselves within these baskets (according to the most common version of events; some variant accounts describe a disguise-based escape amid the confusion of the daily offering ritual) and were carried out of the residence undetected.

What followed was an extraordinarily perilous journey of several weeks across hostile Mughal-controlled territory, largely undertaken in disguise — reportedly at times as wandering religious mendicants (*sadhus*) — avoiding major roads and Mughal patrols, before Shivaji finally reached the safety of Maratha territory in the Deccan by November 1666. For safety, Sambhaji was reportedly sent ahead separately or kept concealed at a trusted location during part of the journey.

The escape became one of the most celebrated episodes of Shivaji's life, cementing his reputation for resourcefulness, courage, and strategic brilliance even under the direst circumstances, and significantly boosting Maratha morale after the setbacks of the preceding two years.

chapters.title: Rebuilding and Renewed Expansion (1667–1670)
chapters.content:
Following his return, Shivaji spent the next several years in a period of careful rebuilding rather than open confrontation, maintaining outward compliance with the Treaty of Purandar for a time while quietly restoring his military and administrative strength.

By 1670, judging the moment right, Shivaji resumed active campaigning, recapturing many of the forts ceded to the Mughals in 1665 — through a mix of assault, negotiation, and, in some cases, purchase from Mughal-appointed custodians. Among the most celebrated recoveries was the Battle of Sinhagad (4 February 1670), in which Shivaji's trusted childhood companion and commander Tanaji Malusare led a daring night assault to recapture the fort of Kondana from its Mughal garrison. Tanaji died in the fierce hand-to-hand fighting that ensued, but the fort was successfully retaken. Deeply moved by his friend's sacrifice, Shivaji renamed the fort Sinhagad ("Lion's Fort") in his honor — it retains this name to the present day.

Later in 1670, Shivaji conducted a second raid on Surat, once again extracting substantial wealth from the city, further funding his military resurgence and signaling to the Mughals that the setbacks of 1665–66 had not permanently checked Maratha ambitions.

chapters.title: Building the Navy and Coastal Power
chapters.content:
A distinctive and historically significant aspect of Shivaji's statecraft was his early and sustained investment in naval power — an area largely neglected by other contemporary Indian land-based powers, including the Mughals.

Recognizing the strategic and commercial importance of the Konkan coastline, and the need to counter the naval presence of the Siddis of Janjira (a coastal power allied at various times with Bijapur and the Mughals), the Portuguese, and English trading interests, Shivaji established dockyards and built a fleet of war vessels, including *gurabs* and *galbats* suited to coastal and riverine warfare.

He fortified strategic coastal and island positions, most notably Sindhudurg, an imposing sea-fort constructed on a rocky island off the Konkan coast beginning around 1664, and Vijaydurg, an older coastal fortress considerably strengthened and expanded under his rule and used as a key naval base. These installations allowed the Marathas to project power along the coast, protect trade, and resist encroachment by rival naval forces — a legacy that would continue to develop under his successors, most notably the later Maratha admiral lineage of the Angre family.

chapters.title: The Coronation at Raigad (1674)
chapters.content:
By the early 1670s, Shivaji presided over a substantial, well-organized, and largely self-sufficient kingdom — complete with an independent treasury, a professional standing army, a functioning navy, and a structured administrative apparatus. Yet in the formal terms recognized by regional and imperial courts of the time, he remained, technically, a rebel chieftain and Mughal or Bijapur vassal rather than a sovereign ruler in his own right — a status that carried real political and diplomatic disadvantages.

To resolve this ambiguity and assert unambiguous, religiously and politically legitimated sovereignty, Shivaji arranged an elaborate formal coronation (*rajyabhishek*) at his capital fort, Raigad, which he had developed as his primary seat of power in place of the earlier capital at Rajgad.

The main coronation ceremony took place on 6 June 1674, presided over by the eminent Varanasi scholar-priest Gaga Bhatt, who had been specially invited to conduct the elaborate Vedic rites — a deliberate choice, as securing a Brahmin of such standing to perform the ceremony helped lend unquestionable religious legitimacy to Shivaji's assumption of sovereign kingship, at a time when some conservative elements questioned the propriety of a Maratha claiming full Kshatriya coronation rites.

Through this ceremony, Shivaji formally adopted the title Chhatrapati ("Lord of the Umbrella," signifying paramount, independent sovereignty) and inaugurated a new calendar era, the Rajyabhishek Shaka, marking the formal establishment of an independent Hindu kingdom in the Deccan — a deeply symbolic assertion of Maratha and Hindu political sovereignty after centuries during which the region had been governed exclusively by Muslim sultanates and the Mughal Empire.

A second coronation ceremony was subsequently conducted later in 1674, following Tantric rites, reportedly at the insistence of Jijabai's family priest and other advisors seeking to incorporate additional regional religious traditions alongside the Vedic ceremony.

Tragically, Jijabai — who had lived to witness the fulfillment of the aspirations she had instilled in her son since childhood — died only weeks after the coronation, in June 1674, a loss that affected Shivaji profoundly.

chapters.title: The Southern Campaign (1676–1679)
chapters.content:
In the final years of his reign, Shivaji turned his attention southward, undertaking an ambitious military and diplomatic campaign into the Carnatic region of South India (roughly corresponding to parts of present-day Karnataka and Tamil Nadu), beginning around 1676–1677.

This campaign served multiple purposes: extending Maratha territorial control and revenue base into the wealthy southern Deccan, securing strategically valuable forts, and addressing family and inheritance matters related to his father Shahaji's estate in the Karnataka region, which was by then held by Shivaji's half-brother, Venkoji (also known as Ekoji), ruler of Thanjavur (Tanjore).

Shivaji's meetings and negotiations with Venkoji were complex, involving both cooperation and tension over the division of their father's territorial legacy, ultimately resulting in an agreement that secured Shivaji's position in parts of the region while formally acknowledging Venkoji's separate rule in Thanjavur — a lineage that would continue as the Thanjavur Maratha kingdom for generations afterward.

By the campaign's conclusion around 1678–1679, Shivaji's kingdom had reached its greatest territorial extent under his direct rule, with an extensive network of forts stretching from the northern Konkan and Deccan down into the Karnataka region.

chapters.title: Administration and Governance in Detail
chapters.content:
Shivaji's system of governance is regarded by historians as remarkably systematic and forward-thinking for its time, reflecting both his own pragmatic instincts and the influence of experienced administrators he retained in his service.

The Ashtapradhan (Council of Eight Ministers), formalized around the time of his coronation, comprised:
1. Peshwa — Chief minister, overseeing general administration
2. Amatya (Majumdar) — Finance minister, responsible for revenue and accounts
3. Sachiv (Chitnis) — Responsible for royal correspondence and records
4. Mantri (Waqenavis) — Responsible for intelligence and record-keeping of court affairs
5. Senapati (Sar-i-Naubat) — Commander-in-chief of the armed forces
6. Sumant (Dabir) — Minister of foreign affairs
7. Nyayadhish — Chief justice, overseeing the judicial system
8. Panditrao — In charge of religious and charitable affairs

This structure ensured a degree of institutional continuity and specialized administration distinct from the more personalized, court-favorite-driven governance common in many contemporary states.

Fort administration: Each fort under Maratha control was typically placed under the joint charge of three officers — a *Havaldar* (fort commander), a *Sabnis* (accountant), and a *Sarnobat* (military officer) — a deliberate arrangement designed to prevent any single individual from gaining unchecked control over a strategic stronghold, reducing the risk of defection or rebellion.

Revenue system: Shivaji implemented systematic land revenue assessment and collection, working to reduce dependence on hereditary intermediaries (deshmukhs and deshpandes) who had often exploited peasants under earlier regimes, and asserting more direct state control over taxation — though hereditary rights were not eliminated entirely, given the political necessity of maintaining the support of established local elites. He also formalized two distinctive revenue claims levied on neighboring, non-Maratha-controlled territories: chauth (a payment, roughly one-quarter of standard revenue, in exchange for protection from Maratha raids) and sardeshmukhi (an additional levy claimed on the basis of a hereditary chieftainship right over the broader Maratha homeland).

Religious policy: Despite his role in reasserting Hindu political sovereignty after centuries of Muslim rule in the Deccan, Shivaji is consistently noted across historical accounts — including some contemporary Persian-language sources critical of him on other grounds — for employing Muslims in significant positions within his administration and military (including in artillery and naval commands), and for issuing explicit instructions to his officers to protect mosques, the Quran, and Muslim non-combatants during military campaigns, distinguishing his conduct from purely sectarian warfare and reflecting a policy grounded in statecraft and justice rather than religious persecution.

chapters.title: Personal Life and Family
chapters.content:
Shivaji had multiple wives, consistent with the customs of contemporary ruling families, including:

Saibai (of the Nimbalkar family) — his first wife, and mother of his son and heir Sambhaji, along with several daughters
Soyarabai (of the Mohite family) — mother of his younger son Rajaram, who would later also become Chhatrapati
Several other wives, including Putalabai, Sakwarbai, and others, in accordance with the marriage-alliance customs common among ruling Maratha families of the period, used partly to cement political relationships with allied clans

His relationship with his mother Jijabai remained the emotional and moral anchor of his life from childhood through her death in 1674, shortly after witnessing his coronation — an event that must have represented, for her, the fulfillment of the values and aspirations she had instilled in him since infancy.

Shivaji's relationship with his elder son and heir, Sambhaji, was at times strained — Sambhaji was known for a more impulsive temperament, and tensions arose over succession politics and court factionalism, particularly involving Soyarabai's faction, which favored her own son Rajaram. Despite periods of friction, Sambhaji accompanied his father on some of the most significant episodes of his life, including the journey to and escape from Agra as a child.

chapters.title: Final Illness and Death (1680)
chapters.content:
In the early months of 1680, after decades of relentless campaigning, administration, and travel across difficult terrain, Shivaji's health began to decline. Historical accounts describe symptoms consistent with prolonged fever, dysentery, and general physical exhaustion, though precise medical details are not recorded with the specificity modern diagnosis would require.

Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj died at his capital fort of Raigad on 3 April 1680, at the age of 50, after a reign that had transformed a fragmented, foreign-dominated region into the nucleus of what would become one of the most significant political powers in early modern Indian history.

His death immediately precipitated a succession crisis. Soyarabai, seeking to secure the throne for her own son, the young Rajaram, moved to have Rajaram enthroned instead of the elder Sambhaji, who was away from Raigad at the time and was regarded with suspicion by parts of the court. Sambhaji, however, acted decisively, securing control of Raigad and the loyalty of key military commanders, and formally ascended the throne as the second Chhatrapati within weeks of his father's death, having Rajaram's regency arrangement overturned.

chapters.title: Legacy
chapters.content:
The kingdom Shivaji founded did not merely survive his death — it expanded dramatically over the following decades, evolving under his successors (Sambhaji, Rajaram, and the later Peshwa administration led by the Bhat family from the early eighteenth century onward) into the Maratha Empire, which by the mid-eighteenth century controlled vast portions of the Indian subcontinent, effectively checked and rolled back Mughal authority across much of India, and remained one of the dominant political and military forces in South Asia until the era of British colonial expansion in the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries.

Shivaji's specific historical contributions are generally summarized by historians as follows:

Military innovation: pioneering effective guerrilla warfare tactics suited to hill and forest terrain, combined with a sophisticated network of hill forts that provided defense in depth against numerically superior conventional armies.
Naval foresight: building one of the earliest significant indigenous Indian naval forces of the early modern period, at a time when most regional land powers neglected sea power entirely.
Administrative innovation: establishing a structured, specialized ministerial system and a revenue administration that reduced (though did not eliminate) dependence on hereditary intermediaries.
Religious policy: maintaining a documented policy of protecting non-combatants and religious sites of other faiths even while militarily and politically reasserting Hindu sovereignty, a nuance frequently highlighted by historians as distinguishing him from purely sectarian conflict narratives.
Symbolic and political legacy: his 1674 coronation as Chhatrapati remains one of the most significant assertions of independent Hindu political sovereignty in the medieval and early modern Deccan, following centuries of rule by external Muslim sultanates and empires.

In Maharashtra and across India today, Shivaji Maharaj remains one of the most widely revered figures in the region's history — commemorated through preserved forts (many now protected heritage sites and popular trekking destinations), statues and memorials, the annual observance of Shiv Jayanti, and an enduring, deeply rooted presence in Marathi cultural identity, literature, and popular memory.

---

*End of Book*

---

A Note on Historical Sources

This account draws on the broad consensus of mainstream historical scholarship on Shivaji Maharaj, synthesized from several types of sources that historians commonly draw upon:

Maratha bakhars (traditional chronicles written in Marathi, some composed during or shortly after Shivaji's lifetime, others compiled later, such as the Sabhasad Bakhar)
Persian-language court chronicles and administrative records from the Mughal and Bijapur courts, which often present more critical or differently framed accounts of the same events
Modern academic historians, including foundational figures such as Jadunath Sarkar and G.S. Sardesai, whose twentieth-century scholarship remains widely cited, alongside more recent historical research

Certain episodes — most notably the precise circumstances of the Afzal Khan meeting, and specific details of the Agra escape — have variant accounts across these different source traditions, as noted within the relevant chapters. Where significant historical debate exists, this book has aimed to present the widely accepted core narrative while flagging areas of documented uncertainty rather than presenting a single account as definitively certain where the historical record itself is contested.

en.title: Ashoka: From Birth to Empire's End
en.summary: The full arc of an emperor's life — from a low-favored prince in Pataliputra, through a bloody rise to the throne, to the war that broke him and the moral empire he built from the wreckage.

chapters.title: Born without sorrow
chapters.content: Ashoka was born around 304 BCE in Pataliputra, the sprawling Mauryan capital on the southern bank of the Ganges, at that time likely one of the largest cities on earth, protected by a timber palisade and moat that Greek visitors to the Mauryan court had described with real astonishment a generation earlier. He was the grandson of Chandragupta Maurya, the soldier-adventurer who, with the guidance of his minister Kautilya, had toppled the Nanda dynasty and pushed back the successors of Alexander the Great to found the Mauryan Empire, and he was the son of the reigning emperor, Bindusara, who had spent his own reign consolidating and quietly expanding what his father had won. Ashoka's mother is remembered by tradition as Dharma, also called Subhadrangi or Janapadakalyani, said to be the daughter of a Brahmin family, though palace politics reportedly kept her at a careful distance from the more favored queens of the royal harem. Because a mother's standing shaped a child's status in the Mauryan court, tradition holds that Ashoka grew up without the automatic advantage enjoyed by sons of more prominent wives — a capable boy, evidently, but not the one anyone would have singled out as the future ruler of the largest empire the subcontinent had yet produced. Even his name carries a story: 'Ashoka' means 'without sorrow,' and later Buddhist legend explains it as his mother's expression of relief at having safely delivered him, though how much of this is memory and how much is invention written centuries later is impossible to say. What is certain is the world he was born into — a court crowded with half-brothers by different mothers, any of whom might, in time, become either an ally or a rival for the throne that would one day govern from the Himalayan foothills to the southern Deccan.

chapters.title: The governor of Ujjain
chapters.content: As a son of the Mauryan royal house, Ashoka would have been raised through the rigorous curriculum expected of a future administrator and soldier: archery and horsemanship, the handling of war elephants, and — perhaps most importantly for a Mauryan prince — grounding in the pragmatic, sometimes ruthless theory of statecraft associated with the Arthashastra, the political treatise linked to his grandfather's minister Kautilya, which treated espionage, taxation, diplomacy, and the calculated use of force as ordinary tools of kingship rather than moral failings. Ashoka appears to have distinguished himself early, and Bindusara rewarded that promise with genuine responsibility rather than ceremonial titles, appointing him governor of the wealthy central Indian region of Avanti, ruling from the city of Ujjain, a major hub of trade routes connecting northern India to the western coast and onward to Persia and beyond. Some traditions place an even earlier posting in his youth at Taxila, in the far northwest near modern Pakistan, a restless frontier city that had a history of revolt; legend holds that Ashoka's mere arrival there, without any need for bloodshed, was enough to bring the unrest to an end, a story likely meant to illustrate both his reputation and his good fortune. It was during his years at Ujjain that Ashoka met Devi, said to be the daughter of a merchant family from the nearby town of Vidisha, and the two were married, apparently outside the arranged, dynastic marriages more typical of a crown prince, suggesting Ashoka's position at the time was not yet seen as central to the succession. Devi bore him two children who would go on to shape the history of Buddhism far beyond India's borders — a son, Mahinda, and a daughter, Sanghamitta — both of whom would later carry their father's faith across the sea to Sri Lanka. Whatever his formal rank at court, Ashoka's years in Avanti built him a genuine record of competent, independent rule, administrative experience that would matter enormously once the question of succession was finally put to the test.

chapters.title: A throne contested
chapters.content: When Bindusara died, conventionally dated to somewhere between 274 and 270 BCE, there was no automatic or peaceful transfer of power. The Mauryan court, like most ancient monarchies without a fixed law of primogeniture, allowed any son with sufficient backing — military, administrative, or personal — to make a claim, and Bindusara had fathered many sons across several wives. Ashoka found himself in direct competition with these half-brothers, and later tradition names the eldest, Susima, as his principal rival for the throne, describing years of maneuvering, alliance-building, and, in the most dramatic Buddhist accounts, outright violence, with Ashoka said to have had a number of his brothers killed to clear his path to power. This period is the origin of the epithet later attached to him in legend — 'Chandashoka,' Ashoka the Fierce or the Cruel — a reputation Buddhist writers were careful to preserve precisely because it made his later transformation seem all the more miraculous. Modern historians read these accounts with real caution: they were composed centuries after the events they describe, by monks whose purpose was moral instruction rather than strict chronicle, and the specific number and manner of these alleged killings cannot be independently verified from Ashoka's own inscriptions, which never mention them. What can be inferred with more confidence is that a genuine and probably extended succession struggle took place, since there appears to have been a gap of several years between Bindusara's death and Ashoka's formal coronation — a gap best explained by the time it likely took him to defeat his rivals, secure the loyalty of the army and the administrative elite in Pataliputra, and consolidate control over a court that had not simply handed him the crown. He was finally crowned emperor around 268 BCE, arriving at the throne of the largest empire in the ancient Indian world not by uncontested inheritance, but by winning a fight that his own later writings would only ever address through silence.

chapters.title: Heir to an unfinished empire
chapters.content: The empire that came under Ashoka's control in 268 BCE was already, by any measure, the most extensive and administratively sophisticated state the Indian subcontinent had ever known, stretching from the mountainous frontier of what is now Afghanistan in the northwest, across the Gangetic plain, and down through most of the peninsula, leaving only a handful of independent kingdoms untouched. It had been built through two generations of deliberate effort: Chandragupta's original conquests and his treaty with the Seleucid successor-king Seleucus I, which brought the Mauryans additional territory in the northwest in exchange for war elephants, and Bindusara's quieter work of consolidation, reportedly extending Mauryan influence further into the Deccan. Ashoka inherited not just territory but a functioning bureaucratic machine — provincial viceroys, often drawn from the royal family itself and stationed at regional capitals such as Taxila, Ujjain, and Tosali near Kalinga; a network of tax collectors and revenue officers; a system of royal roads maintained for both trade and troop movement; and, according to Greek accounts of the Mauryan court, an extensive apparatus of official record-keeping and internal surveillance. In his earliest years as emperor, Ashoka governed very much within this inherited tradition, continuing the martial and centralizing policies of his father and grandfather rather than departing from them. Of the independent territories still outside Mauryan control, one stood out above all others: Kalinga, a prosperous kingdom on the eastern coast in what is now Odisha, wealthy from control of maritime trade routes reaching toward Southeast Asia, and stubborn enough to have resisted absorption even as every neighboring power fell to Mauryan expansion. It represented the last major piece of a subcontinental map his family had spent two generations assembling, and in the eighth year of his reign, Ashoka turned the full weight of the Mauryan military machine toward it.

chapters.title: March to Kalinga
chapters.content: Kalinga was the last major independent kingdom outside Mauryan control, a coastal territory roughly corresponding to modern Odisha and stretching into parts of northern Andhra Pradesh, defended in part by its geography — dense forest and hill country along its inland approaches — and by a population with a long-standing reputation for military resilience. Ashoka's own inscriptions never explain his motive for attacking in the eighth year of his reign, around 261 BCE, and historians have proposed several possibilities without firm resolution: completing the subcontinental unification his grandfather had begun, securing control of Kalinga's lucrative coastal trade routes and access to the sea, or simply continuing an expansionist momentum inherited from his predecessors. Whatever the cause, the campaign that followed was fought with an intensity that set it apart even by the standards of ancient warfare. The Mauryan army, likely drawing on the vast forces described by Greek visitors to the Mauryan court — reportedly including hundreds of thousands of infantry, tens of thousands of cavalry, and thousands of war elephants across the empire as a whole — marched east in full force, and the Kalingans, rather than submitting, mounted a fierce and sustained resistance that dragged the conflict into a full-scale war rather than the brief, decisive campaign the Mauryans may have expected. Entire regions of the kingdom became battlegrounds, with fighting spilling beyond the professional armies to engulf towns, farmland, and civilian populations across the contested territory. By the strict measure of military outcome, it was an unambiguous Mauryan victory — Kalinga was defeated, and its territory absorbed permanently into the empire — but Ashoka himself, standing amid the aftermath, found he could not describe what he had achieved there in the language of triumph that any king before him would have used without hesitation.

chapters.title: The silence after war
chapters.content: One hundred thousand dead. One hundred and fifty thousand more forcibly deported from their homes. Uncounted further thousands lost in the months that followed to the famine and disease that inevitably follow the devastation of farmland, granaries, and settlements in wartime. These figures are not later scholarly estimates pieced together from fragments — they come directly from Ashoka's own hand, carved years afterward into his Thirteenth Major Rock Edict, one of the most extraordinary documents to survive from the ancient world precisely because a reigning, victorious monarch chose to record the human cost of his own war rather than its glory. In that inscription, Ashoka goes further than simply listing casualties: he describes his own remorse and grief explicitly, and — unusually for any ruler of the period — extends his sorrow beyond the soldiers who fell in battle to the Brahmins, ascetics, householders, and ordinary families of Kalinga who suffered injury, separation from loved ones, or loss regardless of whether they had taken any part in the fighting at all. Buddhist legend, composed centuries later, supplies a vivid narrative to accompany these bare facts: Ashoka walking through the ruined battlefield and city in the campaign's aftermath, confronted by corpses left unburied, by the wailing of newly made widows and orphaned children, and by a once-flourishing land reduced to wreckage, and finding that something in him broke open that could not be repaired by declaring victory. Whatever precisely passed through the emperor's mind in those weeks cannot be reconstructed with certainty, but the outcome is beyond dispute because Ashoka states it himself, in his own words, carved permanently into rock: Kalinga was the last war of conquest he would ever wage, and the grief it produced became the hinge on which the rest of his reign, and much of ancient Indian history, would turn.

chapters.title: Conquest by Dhamma
chapters.content: In the years that followed Kalinga, Ashoka articulated something no ruler on the Indian subcontinent is known to have declared before him: an explicit, public renunciation of war as an acceptable instrument of state expansion. In edict after edict, he drew a deliberate and repeated distinction between what he called digvijaya, 'conquest by war,' the path he had walked to Kalinga, and dhamma-vijaya, 'conquest by Dhamma,' the path he now committed himself to instead — the aspiration to draw other peoples toward his values not through the threat of the sword but through moral example, generosity, and the visible good conduct of his own administration. This was not, it should be said plainly, a wholesale abandonment of imperial power: Ashoka did not disband the Mauryan army, did not withdraw from Kalinga or return its independence, and continued to govern an empire that remained, by any definition, one built originally through conquest. What changed was forward-looking and deliberate — a formal renunciation of further military expansion, and a redirection of the emperor's considerable energy, resources, and administrative reach toward the welfare of the people already under his rule. He described this new orientation using the term Dhamma, a Prakrit rendering of the Sanskrit Dharma, encompassing a broad ethical program of honesty, compassion, self-restraint, respect for elders and religious teachers of every tradition, and humane treatment of servants, slaves, and even animals. It represented less a rejection of imperial ambition than its complete redefinition — a sovereign choosing, for the first time in recorded Indian history, to measure the success of his reign not by the size of the map he controlled, but by the moral and material condition of the people living within it.

chapters.title: A pilgrim's faith
chapters.content: Ashoka's embrace of Buddhism was not, according to his own account, an instantaneous conversion in the aftermath of Kalinga but rather a gradual deepening of commitment over several years, and his edicts describe this evolution with unusual candor: he refers to himself first simply as an upasaka, an ordinary lay follower, and only later notes that his devotion grew markedly more 'zealous' and active after a period of comparatively casual affiliation. As his faith intensified, Ashoka undertook pilgrimages to the most sacred sites associated with the Buddha's life, most notably Bodh Gaya, where the Buddha was said to have attained enlightenment beneath the Bodhi Tree, and Lumbini, in the Himalayan foothills of what is now Nepal, traditionally identified as the Buddha's birthplace. At Lumbini, Ashoka left behind one of the very few acts of his reign that can be dated with real historical precision: a stone pillar, still standing today, inscribed to commemorate his personal visit to the site and to grant the local village a reduction in its tax obligations in honor of the occasion — a small, concrete act of royal generosity that survives independently of legend. Later Buddhist tradition supplies richer, though far less verifiable, detail about this period of his life, crediting monks such as Upagupta or the elder Nigrodha with instructing and guiding him toward a fuller understanding of Buddhist teaching, and describing Ashoka's active patronage of the monastic community, the sangha, including significant donations toward its support and construction of monasteries. Whatever the precise sequence of events, the trajectory recorded in Ashoka's own words is unmistakable: a man who had once commanded the deaths of a hundred thousand people was, over the course of years rather than a single dramatic reversal, deliberately and methodically rebuilding his life, and eventually his entire theory of kingship, around a philosophy of restraint, compassion, and non-violence.

chapters.title: Dhamma across the empire
chapters.content: Ashoka turned his empire toward dhamma — tolerance, non-violence, care for animals and servants, and respect across faiths — weaving these principles into an ambitious and unusually concrete program of governance unlike anything the ancient world had previously seen attempted by a reigning monarch. His edicts describe the construction of hospitals and medical treatment centers for both human beings and animals, and he claims, in one inscription, to have arranged for medicinal herbs and roots to be cultivated and made available not only within his own territory but even among neighboring peoples beyond direct Mauryan rule, an early and remarkable claim of cross-border humanitarian provision. Along the empire's roads, he ordered the planting of shade-giving trees and mango groves for the comfort of travelers, the digging of wells at regular intervals to ensure access to water, and the construction of rest houses where people and pack animals alike could shelter during long journeys. Within the palace itself, he sharply curtailed the killing of animals for the royal kitchen, restricted or eliminated many of the elaborate hunting expeditions that had served as royal entertainment for generations of Mauryan kings before him, and issued specific regulations protecting certain species from slaughter altogether, alongside broader prohibitions against cruelty to animals of all kinds — a body of policy that some modern historians have described as an unusually early expression of environmental and animal-welfare ethics articulated by a head of state. His ambitions reached well beyond his own borders: his edicts record having sent emissaries carrying his message of Dhamma as far as the Hellenistic kingdoms of the eastern Mediterranean, naming specific rulers of the era, including Antiochus II of the Seleucid realm, Ptolemy II of Egypt, Antigonus II Gonatas of Macedon, Magas of Cyrene, and Alexander of Epirus, as recipients of missions intended to spread not territorial ambition but medical knowledge, ethical instruction, and diplomatic goodwill — evidence, extraordinary for its time, of Mauryan India's active awareness of and outreach toward the wider Mediterranean world in the third century BCE.

chapters.title: Officers of the moral law
chapters.content: To ensure that Dhamma amounted to more than words carved into distant rock faces, Ashoka established an entirely new tier of imperial administration: the Dhamma Mahamatras, officials specifically charged with carrying his ethical program into the everyday life of the empire's provinces. These officers were tasked with traveling regularly through their assigned territories to oversee the fair and humane treatment of prisoners, to check on the welfare of the elderly, the poor, and those without family support, and to instruct local populations directly in the principles of conduct Ashoka wished to see practiced throughout his realm. Ashoka also transformed his own personal conduct as a ruler to reflect these values: where earlier Mauryan kings, including his own father and grandfather, had traditionally undertaken hunting expeditions as a form of royal leisure and display, Ashoka replaced these with journeys he called dhamma-yatras, tours during which he visited ascetics and religious teachers of various traditions, distributed gifts and alms, met directly with ordinary subjects, and personally delivered moral instruction along the way. His edicts also record specific reforms to judicial practice: he instructed officers of the court to act with impartiality regardless of the social standing of those before them, ordered periodic review of prisoners' cases by specially appointed officials, and — in a provision remarkable for its humanity in the ancient world — decreed that individuals sentenced to death be granted a period of respite, reportedly several days, during which family members could appeal on their behalf or the condemned could make offerings and prepare for death with dignity rather than facing immediate execution. Taken together, these institutional innovations reveal a ruler attempting something highly unusual for his era: translating a personal ethical and religious transformation into durable, functioning instruments of state administration rather than leaving it as private conviction alone.

chapters.title: Words carved to outlast empires
chapters.content: Across the vast territory of his empire, Ashoka ordered his reflections, policies, and personal reckonings carved permanently into rock faces and specially quarried sandstone pillars, ensuring that his subjects, wherever they lived and whatever language they spoke, could encounter his words directly rather than through intermediaries. The result, pieced together by modern archaeology and epigraphy, is a body of roughly thirty-three surviving inscriptions: fourteen Major Rock Edicts found at sites scattered from the northwest frontier to the southern Deccan, a further set of Minor Rock Edicts, and seven Pillar Edicts inscribed on monolithic columns — remarkable feats of engineering in their own right, some standing between forty and fifty feet tall, quarried, polished to a glassy sheen using techniques not fully replicated again for centuries, and transported, in some cases, hundreds of miles to the sites where they were erected. Most were composed in Prakrit using the Brahmi script, but Ashoka's government showed a striking sensitivity to linguistic diversity within the empire: inscriptions in the northwest frontier region were rendered in the Kharoshthi script, and near Kandahar in modern Afghanistan, edicts survive in both Greek and Aramaic, the languages of the substantial Hellenistic and Persian-descended populations living under Mauryan rule in that region. For nearly two thousand years after the fall of the Mauryan Empire, these inscriptions sat largely unread, their script forgotten along with the political order that had produced it, and Ashoka's own historical memory survived only faintly, preserved mainly as a bare name in old Puranic king-lists and in a body of Buddhist legendary literature that few outside the monastic tradition encountered. It was not until 1837 that the British scholar and orientalist James Prinsep, working from inscriptions found on the Sanchi stupa and elsewhere, finally succeeded in deciphering the Brahmi script, allowing historians for the first time to connect the mysterious royal title 'Devanampiya Piyadasi' found throughout the inscriptions to the Ashoka named in the old dynastic lists — and, in doing so, to hear the emperor's own voice, describing his own war, his own remorse, and his own vision of good governance, for the first time in nearly two thousand years.

chapters.title: Shadows in the palace
chapters.content: The final decades of Ashoka's life are considerably harder to trace with confidence than his early and middle reign, and much of what survives about this period comes from later legendary tradition rather than his own contemporary inscriptions. Beyond Devi, whom he had married during his years as governor of Avanti, later tradition names several additional wives, including a chief queen, Asandhimitra, who is said to have held a position of particular prominence for much of his reign, and a considerably younger queen, Tishyarakshita, who appears in the Buddhist storytelling tradition in a strikingly unflattering light. According to the Sanskrit legendary text known as the Ashokavadana, Tishyarakshita, consumed by jealousy over Ashoka's deep personal devotion to the Bodhi Tree at Bodh Gaya, arranged to have the sacred tree destroyed — a story the Sri Lankan chronicle tradition tells somewhat differently, with the tree's damage eventually leading to a cutting from it being transported and replanted in Sri Lanka, where a tree said to descend from it is still venerated today. The same body of legend preserves the poignant story of a son named Kunala, celebrated in tradition for the remarkable beauty of his eyes, who is said to have been blinded through court intrigue attributed to Tishyarakshita, motivated by resentment or fear regarding his position in the succession; Kunala's response to this cruelty — accepting his blindness without bitterness and continuing to express devotion and forgiveness rather than seeking revenge — became, in Buddhist retelling, a celebrated parable of patience and non-attachment. Historians treat these accounts with considerable caution, recognizing them as moral literature composed generations after the fact rather than verified court record, but many nonetheless suspect that beneath the legendary embellishment lies some genuine memory of real tension within the royal household as Ashoka aged, his health presumably declined, and the always-difficult question of who would succeed him grew steadily more urgent.

chapters.title: The emperor's last years
chapters.content: As Ashoka's long reign entered its final stretch, the pace and frequency of his surviving edicts noticeably decline, and the historical record of his day-to-day governance grows correspondingly thin, leaving historians to rely once again on legendary sources of uncertain reliability to fill the gap. Some later Buddhist accounts claim that Ashoka's ministers, or possibly members of his own extended family, grew increasingly uneasy at the sheer scale of his continued religious donations to the Buddhist sangha in his final years, to the point that his practical control over the royal treasury was deliberately curtailed by those around him — a story sometimes framed in Buddhist tradition as evidence of Ashoka's total, self-sacrificing devotion to the faith, since he is said to have continued giving away what little remained within his reach even after losing broader authority. Whether this reflects an authentic political struggle in the final years of his rule, or is instead a pious exaggeration crafted by later Buddhist writers to emphasize the completeness of the emperor's generosity, cannot be determined with confidence from the surviving evidence, since no contemporary inscription addresses the matter directly. What can be said with somewhat more assurance is that the extraordinary institutional energy that had defined the middle decades of his reign — the steady output of new edicts, the expansion of public works, the dispatch of missions to distant courts — appears to have slowed considerably in these last years, consistent with the gradual decline in vigor one would expect of a ruler by then well advanced in age, after already governing for well over three decades.

chapters.title: Death of an emperor
chapters.content: Ashoka is generally believed to have died around 232 BCE, though, as with the date of his birth, the precise year cannot be fixed with certainty, and different scholarly traditions place his death anywhere between roughly 238 and 232 BCE depending on how various regnal-year references in the edicts and later king-lists are reconciled. By the most widely accepted reckoning, he had reigned for approximately thirty-six to thirty-seven years, a remarkably long tenure by the standards of ancient monarchy anywhere in the world, during which he had fundamentally reshaped both the administrative machinery and the underlying moral purpose of the state he inherited from his father and grandfather. And yet, in a silence that stands in striking contrast to the vast textual record he left behind describing his war, his remorse, and his program of reform, neither Ashoka's own inscriptions nor any surviving contemporary source offers a detailed account of his final illness, the precise circumstances of his death, or the manner of his passing — an unusual gap for a ruler who had spent the better part of four decades ensuring that his voice, in stone, would outlast him by millennia. Later Buddhist legendary literature offers only scattered and largely unverifiable fragments concerning his final days, none of which historians treat as reliable narrative history, leaving the actual death of one of ancient India's most extensively self-documented rulers as, paradoxically, one of the least documented moments of his entire life.

chapters.title: An empire dissolves, a legacy endures
chapters.content: Whatever stability and unity Ashoka had provided during his long reign did not survive him for long. In the years immediately following his death, succession disputes among his descendants — combined with the inherent difficulty of holding together an empire as vast, ethnically diverse, and administratively demanding as the one the Mauryans had built — began to erode central authority, and within roughly fifty years the empire had effectively fractured beyond meaningful repair, with the core Magadhan territory eventually passing to the Shunga dynasty around 185 BCE, an event tradition associates with the assassination of the last Mauryan ruler. For nearly two thousand years afterward, Ashoka's specific historical memory faded almost entirely from view outside the Buddhist monastic tradition that continued to revere him as a model royal patron; his great stone pillars stood weathering and largely unread across the subcontinent, his stupas gradually vanished beneath accumulated earth and encroaching forest, and the very script in which he had recorded his life's work became, for all practical purposes, a forgotten language. Yet the physical legacy endured even as the name attached to it slipped from common memory, and when the modern nation of India took shape in the twentieth century, it deliberately reached back across those intervening centuries to reclaim him as a founding symbol. The Lion Capital that had once crowned Ashoka's pillar at Sarnath, marking the site of the Buddha's first sermon, was formally adopted as the National Emblem of independent India, and the wheel carved beneath those four lions — the Ashoka Chakra — was placed at the very center of the Indian national flag, where it turns today, largely unremarked, in the daily life of over a billion people, a permanent reminder of a third-century BCE emperor who once stood amid the wreckage of a war he had decisively won and concluded, against every precedent of the age he lived in, that winning was not enough.

mr.title: छत्रपती शिवाजी महाराज: एका योद्ध्या राजाचे जीवन
mr.summary: तरुण शिवाजी महाराजांनी किल्ले उभारले, युती जोडल्या आणि स्वराज्याची नवी कल्पना साकार केली, याचा एक चित्तवेधक आढावा.

chapters.title: शिवाजी महाराजांच्या आधीचा दख्खन
chapters.content:
शिवाजी महाराजांनी काय साध्य केले, याची खरी व्याप्ती समजून घेण्यासाठी आधी ते ज्या खंडित आणि परकीय सत्तेखालील जगात जन्माला आले, ते समजून घेणे आवश्यक आहे.

सतराव्या शतकाच्या सुरुवातीस, दख्खनचे पठार — म्हणजे आजच्या महाराष्ट्र, कर्नाटक आणि तेलंगणाचा बराचसा भाग व्यापणारा विस्तीर्ण भूप्रदेश — पंधराव्या शतकाच्या अखेरीस विभाजित झालेल्या जुन्या बहमनी सल्तनतीच्या तीन प्रमुख मुस्लिम सल्तनतींमध्ये विभागलेले होते:

अहमदनगर सल्तनत (निजामशाही घराणे), पश्चिम महाराष्ट्राचा बराचसा भाग नियंत्रित करणारी
विजापूर सल्तनत (आदिलशाही घराणे), नैऋत्य दख्खन आणि कर्नाटकाचा काही भाग नियंत्रित करणारी
गोवळकोंडा सल्तनत (कुतुबशाही घराणे), आजच्या हैदराबादच्या परिसरातील पूर्व दख्खन नियंत्रित करणारी

उत्तरेकडे, मुघल साम्राज्य — त्यावेळी सम्राट शाहजहानच्या आणि १६५८ पासून औरंगजेबाच्या कारकिर्दीत सामर्थ्याच्या शिखरावर — दक्षिणेकडे सातत्याने आपला विस्तार करत होते. १६३६ सालापर्यंत त्यांनी अहमदनगर पूर्णपणे गिळंकृत केले आणि विजापूर व गोवळकोंड्यावर सतत लष्करी व राजनैतिक दबाव कायम ठेवला.

मराठे — सह्याद्रीच्या डोंगराळ प्रदेशातील आणि कोकण किनारपट्टीतील मराठी भाषिक समुदाय — यांच्याकडे स्वतःची कोणतीही स्वतंत्र राजकीय सत्ता नव्हती. त्याऐवजी, मराठा घराणी विविध सल्तनतींच्या सेवेत जहागीरदार (लष्करी सेवेच्या मोबदल्यात जमीन अनुदान धारण करणारे), देशमुख (वंशपरंपरागत स्थानिक प्रशासक व महसूल गोळा करणारे), आणि घोडदळाचे सेनापती म्हणून कार्यरत होती. भोसले, जाधव, मोरे, घाटगे, निंबाळकर यांसारखी प्रमुख मराठा घराणी अनेकदा एकाच संघर्षात परस्परविरोधी बाजूंनी लढत असत — ज्या सुलतानाच्या सेवेत असत त्याच्या बाजूने, आणि कधीकधी आपापसातही.

हे जग निष्ठा सतत बदलणे, दरबारी कारस्थाने, आणि दख्खनमध्ये एकसंध प्रादेशिक अस्मिता किंवा हिंदू राजकीय सार्वभौमत्वाचा जवळजवळ पूर्ण अभाव यांनी वेढलेले होते — अशी स्थिती या भागातील पूर्वीची हिंदू राज्ये लयाला गेल्यापासून शतकानुशतके टिकून होती. याच खंडित पार्श्वभूमीवर शिवाजी महाराजांनी नंतर साध्य केलेली कामगिरी — म्हणजे एक स्वतंत्र हिंदवी स्वराज्य उभारून त्याचा औपचारिक राज्याभिषेक करणे — याला तिचे पूर्ण ऐतिहासिक महत्त्व प्राप्त होते.

chapters.title: वंशावळ आणि कौटुंबिक पार्श्वभूमी
chapters.content:
शिवाजी महाराजांचे घराणे, भोसले कुळ, मेवाडच्या सिसोदिया राजपुतांचे वंशज असल्याचा दावा करत असे. क्षत्रिय वंशाची वैधता मिळवू पाहणाऱ्या अनेक मराठा घराण्यांमध्ये असा दावा सामान्य होता, आणि आधुनिक इतिहासकार त्याकडे काहीशा साशंकतेने पाहतात — दख्खनमधील लष्करी घराण्यांच्या त्या काळातील व्यापक वंशनिर्मिती परंपरेचा तो एक भाग मानला जातो.

त्यांचे आजोबा, मालोजी भोसले, तुलनेने सामान्य पार्श्वभूमीतून पुढे येऊन अहमदनगर सल्तनतीच्या सेवेत एक महत्त्वाचे जहागीरदार बनले. त्यांनी पुणे आणि सुपे या भागांचे किल्ले व महसूल हक्क मिळवले आणि निजामशाही दरबाराकडून त्यांना "राजा" ही पदवी बहाल करण्यात आली. या उदयामुळेच त्यांचा मुलगा शहाजी हा आपल्या पिढीतील सर्वांत सामर्थ्यशाली मराठा सेनापतींपैकी एक बनण्याचा पाया रचला गेला.

शिवाजी महाराजांचे वडील शहाजी भोसले हे अत्यंत कुशल सेनापती होते, ज्यांनी आपल्या कारकिर्दीत वेगवेगळ्या टप्प्यांवर अहमदनगर सल्तनत, मुघल साम्राज्य आणि शेवटी विजापूर सल्तनत यांची सेवा केली — त्याकाळच्या मराठा लष्करी सेवेतील अस्थिर, व्यवहारकेंद्री स्वरूपाचे हे प्रतिबिंब होते. १६३०च्या दशकात मुघलांनी अहमदनगर जिंकल्यानंतर, शहाजींनी काही काळ एका लहान राजपुत्राला गादीवर बसवून निजामशाही टिकवण्याचा प्रयत्न केला आणि माहुली किल्ल्यावरून प्रतिकार चालू ठेवला, पण अखेर सुमारे १६३६ साली त्यांना शरणागती पत्करून विजापूरच्या सेवेत जावे लागले. विजापूरने त्यांना पुणे परिसरात तसेच कर्नाटक भागात (बंगळूर) मोठी जहागीर बहाल केली, जिथे त्यांनी आपल्या पुढील कारकिर्दीचा बराचसा काळ घालवला.

शिवाजी महाराजांची आई, जिजाबाई, सिंदखेडच्या जाधव घराण्यातून आल्या होत्या — हेदेखील एक प्रतिष्ठित मराठा लष्करी घराणे, ज्याने विविध वेळी निजामशाही व मुघल दरबारांची सेवा केली होती. त्या आपल्या गाढ धार्मिक श्रद्धेसाठी, खंबीर स्वभावासाठी आणि कठीण परिस्थितीतही प्रतिष्ठा व हिंदू परंपरा जपण्याच्या आग्रहासाठी ओळखल्या जात — याच गुणांनी पुढे त्यांच्या मुलाचे व्यक्तिमत्त्व खोलवर घडवले.

शहाजी आणि जिजाबाई यांचा विवाह, त्या काळातील प्रथेनुसार दोन संलग्न लष्करी घराण्यांमध्ये ठरवून झाला होता. या विवाहातून अनेक अपत्ये जन्माला आली, परंतु त्या काळातील वास्तवानुसार अनेक अपत्ये बालपणीच दगावली. १६३० साली जन्मलेले शिवाजी हे वाचलेल्या अपत्यांपैकी एक होते, तसेच त्यांना संभाजी नावाचे मोठे बंधूही होते (शिवाजी महाराजांच्या स्वतःच्या मुलाशी, म्हणजे संभाजी महाराजांशी, गल्लत करू नये) — जे मुख्यतः शहाजींसोबत बंगळूरच्या जहागिरीत वाढले आणि १६५४ साली विजापूरच्या सेवेत असताना दख्खनमधील एका लढाईत मृत्युमुखी पडले.

chapters.title: शिवनेरी येथे जन्म (१६३०)
chapters.content:
शिवाजी महाराजांचा जन्म १९ फेब्रुवारी १६३० रोजी पुण्याजवळील जुन्नर या रांगड्या डोंगराळ प्रदेशातील शिवनेरी किल्ल्यावर झाला. ही निश्चित तारीख काहीशी वादग्रस्त मानली जाते, कारण पारंपरिक मराठी संदर्भ ही तारीख हिंदू पंचांगानुसार (फाल्गुन वद्य तृतीया, शके १५५१) नोंदवतात, जी गणनापद्धतीनुसार इंग्रजी दिनदर्शिकेत किंचित वेगवेगळ्या तारखांशी जुळते. तरीही १९ फेब्रुवारी १६३० ही तारीख आधुनिक इतिहासकारांमध्ये सर्वाधिक मान्यताप्राप्त असून महाराष्ट्रात ती अधिकृतपणे "शिवजयंती" म्हणून साजरी केली जाते.

शिवनेरी हे जन्मस्थान ठरण्यामागे तत्कालीन परिस्थिती कारणीभूत होती: शहाजी सतत लष्करी मोहिमांमध्ये आणि प्रदेशातील राजकीय अस्थिरतेत गुंतलेले असल्यामुळे (हाच तो काळ होता जेव्हा अहमदनगर सल्तनत मुघल दबावाखाली कोसळत होती), जिजाबाईंनी शहाजींच्या अखत्यारीतील या सुरक्षित डोंगरी किल्ल्याचा आश्रय घेतला होता.

परंपरेनुसार, जिजाबाईंनी किल्ल्याची स्थानिक कुलदेवता शिवाई देवीकडे निरोगी पुत्रप्राप्तीसाठी प्रार्थना केली होती, आणि त्याच देवीच्या सन्मानार्थ आपल्या नवजात मुलाचे नाव शिवाजी ठेवले. आजही शिवनेरी किल्ल्यावर शिवाई देवीचे मंदिर अस्तित्वात आहे, आणि हा किल्ला एक संरक्षित ऐतिहासिक वास्तू म्हणून जतन केला गेला आहे, जिथे विशेषतः शिवजयंतीच्या काळात मोठ्या संख्येने भाविक व पर्यटक भेट देतात.

chapters.title: जिजाबाई आणि दादोजी कोंडदेव यांच्या मार्गदर्शनाखालील बालपण
chapters.content:
शहाजींची कारकीर्द त्यांना अधिकाधिक विजापूरच्या सेवेत कर्नाटक प्रदेशाकडे घेऊन गेली, आणि सुमारे १६३६ साली त्यांनी जिजाबाई व लहान शिवाजी यांना पुण्यात स्थायिक होण्याची व्यवस्था केली — पूर्वीच्या दशकातील युद्धांमुळे उद्ध्वस्त झालेली ही जहागीर होती. या मालमत्तेचे व्यवस्थापन आणि शिवाजींचे संगोपन व शिक्षण यांची जबाबदारी त्यांनी एका विश्वासू व अत्यंत कुशल प्रशासक, दादोजी कोंडदेव, यांच्याकडे सोपवली.

शिवाजी महाराजांचे व्यक्तिमत्त्व घडवण्यात जिजाबाईंची भूमिका जवळजवळ सर्वच इतिहासकार आणि पारंपरिक संदर्भ त्यांच्या बालपणातील सर्वांत मूलभूत प्रभाव मानतात. पतीच्या जवळजवळ कायमस्वरूपी अनुपस्थितीमुळे आणि युद्धग्रस्त जहागिरीचे व्यवस्थापन करण्याच्या जबाबदारीमुळे सर्वसामान्य गृहस्थ जीवनापासून वंचित राहिलेल्या जिजाबाईंनी शिवाजींना ठाम नैतिक आणि धार्मिक बैठकीसह वाढवण्यासाठी स्वतःला वाहून घेतले. त्यांनी शिवाजींना रामायण, महाभारत, तसेच ऐतिहासिक हिंदू राजे व वीरांच्या कथा विस्ताराने सांगितल्या, ज्यातून त्यांच्यात न्याय्य राज्यकारभाराची (धर्माची) मूल्ये, धैर्य, आणि आपल्या प्रजेचे व श्रद्धेचे रक्षण करण्याची जाणीव रुजली. त्यांनी स्थानिक मंदिरे आणि धार्मिक व्यक्तींशीही घनिष्ठ संबंध राखले, ज्यामुळे अशा काळात, जेव्हा काही प्रादेशिक मुस्लिम राज्यकर्त्यांच्या अंमलाखाली मंदिरांची तोडफोड व धार्मिक भेदभावाच्या नोंदी आढळतात (जरी धोरणे राज्यकर्त्यांनुसार आणि काळानुसार लक्षणीयरीत्या वेगवेगळी होती), आपल्या मुलामध्ये सशक्त हिंदू सांस्कृतिक अस्मिता अधिक दृढ झाली.

दादोजी कोंडदेव यांनी पुणे जहागिरीच्या प्रत्यक्ष पुनर्बांधणीची जबाबदारी सांभाळली — शेतकऱ्यांचे पुनर्वसन, सिंचन व शेतीची पुनर्बांधणी, आणि निजामशाही, विजापूर व मुघल सैन्यांमधील वर्षानुवर्षांच्या युद्धांमुळे उद्ध्वस्त झालेल्या प्रदेशात कायदा व सुव्यवस्था पुन्हा प्रस्थापित करणे. त्यांच्या देखरेखीखाली शिवाजींना वाचन-लेखन, अश्वारोहण, तलवारबाजी, कुस्ती, आणि राज्यकारभाराच्या मूलभूत तत्त्वांचे प्रशिक्षण मिळाले. काही पारंपरिक संदर्भांमध्ये संस्कृत आणि धार्मिक ग्रंथांचे औपचारिक शिक्षणही नमूद आहे, जरी शिवाजींच्या औपचारिक साक्षरतेची व्याप्ती इतिहासकारांमध्ये चर्चेचा विषय आहे — काही समकालीन संदर्भांनुसार ते अस्खलितपणे वाचू-लिहू शकत नसत आणि लेखनिकांवर अवलंबून असत. मात्र यामुळे त्यांच्या राज्यकारभार व रणनीतीवरील असामान्य प्रभुत्वात कोणतीही उणीव आली नाही.

महत्त्वाचे म्हणजे, शिवाजींचे बालपण केवळ दरबारी जीवनापुरते मर्यादित नव्हते. त्यांनी मावळ प्रदेशातील कणखर डोंगरी समुदायांसोबत — शेतकरी, मेंढपाळ आणि सह्याद्रीच्या कठीण भूप्रदेशाची सवय असलेले शिकारी — बराच वेळ घालवला, आणि तानाजी मालुसरे, येसाजी कंक, बाजी पासलकर, सूर्याजी मालुसरे यांसारख्या मुलांशी घनिष्ठ मैत्री जोडली. जात किंवा वंशपरंपरागत दर्जावर आधारित नसून समान संगोपनावर आधारित असलेली ही सुरुवातीची नाती, पुढे शिवाजींनी आपल्या लष्करी मोहिमांमध्ये ज्या तीव्र वैयक्तिक निष्ठेचा आधार घेतला, त्याचा पाया ठरली. एका सरदारपुत्रासाठी असामान्य असलेली ही भूप्रदेश आणि सामान्य जनतेशी असलेली मूलभूत जवळीक, शिवाजींच्या नंतरच्या लष्करी दृष्टिकोनाला पारंपरिक दख्खनी सरदारवर्गाच्या दृष्टिकोनापेक्षा वेगळे ठरवणारा एक महत्त्वाचा घटक म्हणून इतिहासकार वारंवार नमूद करतात.
